did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used AI methods including Vision Transformers (ViTs), XGBoost, and EfficientNet-B3 for skin cancer classification on the HAM10000 dataset, integrated with Explainable AI (XAI) techniques such as Grad-CAM, SHAP, and LIME to enhance interpretability.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work, including the development of an AI framework using ViTs, XGBoost, and EfficientNet-B3 on the HAM10000 dataset, along with the application of XAI techniques (Grad-CAM, SHAP, LIME) and reporting of specific performance metrics (81.3% accuracy, 91.28% AUC). Although it contains a literature review section, its primary purpose is to report new results from a proposed methodology, classifying it as a research paper (specifically a conference paper).

title: Enhancing Skin Cancer Diagnosis with XAI: Integrating Grad-CAM, SHAP, and LIME for Model Interpretability
brief_summary: This study addresses the opacity of AI models in skin cancer detection by developing an explainable framework using the HAM10000 dataset. The researchers employed Vision Transformers (ViTs) for classification, integrating Grad-CAM for visual interpretability, alongside SHAP for XGBoost and LIME for EfficientNet-B3. The ViT model was trained from scratch on pre-processed dermoscopic images, achieving a test accuracy of 81.3% and an AUC of 91.28%. The integration of these XAI techniques provides clinicians with both visual heatmaps and quantitative insights into model decision-making. The results demonstrate that combining high-performance modeling with explainability tools can enhance trust and reliability in diagnostic workflows.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two major lesion types filtered from the HAM10000 dataset for the classification task. It was the minority class in the imbalanced dataset, addressed via random over-sampling.
One of the two major lesion types filtered from the HAM10000 dataset for the classification task. It was the majority class in the imbalanced dataset.
Mentioned as one of the seven skin lesion categories present in the full HAM10000 dataset, but not included in the specific binary classification experiment which focused only on melanoma and nevus.
Mentioned in the introduction as a general classification category of skin cancer, but not used as a specific label in the experimental dataset filtering.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into Melanoma and Melanocytic Nevus classes
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification using the HAM10000 dataset, specifically filtering for two classes: Melanoma (MEL) and Melanocytic Nevus (NV). The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (benign vs. malignant) with a reported test accuracy of 81.3% and an AUC of 91.28%. The confusion matrix reports true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives, confirming a classification task.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Human Against Machine with 10,000 training images)
datasets_used.description: A public benchmark dataset of high-resolution dermoscopic images representing common pigmented skin lesions, compiled to support machine learning research for automated skin cancer detection. It was accessed via Kaggle. The study filtered this dataset to include only two specific lesion types.

use.training: Used for training the Vision Transformer (ViT), XGBoost, and EfficientNet-B3 models. The dataset was pre-processed (resized to 256x256, null age values removed/mean imputed) and balanced using random over-sampling of the minority class (Melanoma) to match the majority class (Nevus).
use.validation: Used for validation during model training (loss curves were monitored).
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the model, yielding a test accuracy of 81.3% and an AUC of 91.28%.
use.other: Used for Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) to analyze demographic distributions (age, gender, localization) and class imbalance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reported descriptive statistics (e.g., mean age, gender distribution) and model performance metrics (accuracy, AUC, confusion matrix counts) without inferential statistical comparisons.

XGBoost.notes: Quantitative interpretability provided via SHAP; specific metric values not reported in text.

EfficientNet-B3.notes: Predictions demystified via LIME; specific metric values not reported in text.

HAM10000.notes: The ViT model was trained on a dataset filtered for Melanoma and Melanocytic Nevus classes, balanced via random over-sampling. The model achieved high reliability in classification. XAI techniques (Grad-CAM for ViT, SHAP for XGBoost, LIME for EfficientNet-B3) were integrated to provide visual and quantitative explanations.

architecture_details.normalization: Global layer normalization before attention/MLP operations
architecture_details.optimization_techniques: Layer normalization before attention/MLP operations for training stability

training_details.data_augmentation: Random horizontal flipping and random rotation
training_details.dataset_balancing: Random over-sampling of minority class (Melanoma) to match majority class (Nevus)

classification_or_output_head.notes: Compressed classification head to prevent overfitting

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during training to enhance model generalization and prevent overfitting, implemented via TensorFlow's tf.keras.layers.experimental.preprocessing API.

Used to balance the class distribution by enhancing the number of samples in the minority class (Melanoma) until it equals the majority class (Melanocytic Nevus).

conclusions: The study concludes that Vision Transformers (ViTs) offer superior contextual understanding compared to Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for skin cancer classification, particularly in handling imbalanced datasets.

The proposed framework achieved a test accuracy of 81.3% and an Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 91.28% on the HAM10000 dataset, demonstrating robust classification capability for distinguishing between Melanoma and Melanocytic Nevus.

Integrating Explainable AI (XAI) techniques—specifically Grad-CAM for ViTs, SHAP for XGBoost, and LIME for EfficientNet-B3—bridges the gap between predictive performance and interpretability, providing clinicians with visual heatmaps and quantitative insights.
The use of XAI tools enhances trust and reliability in AI-driven diagnostic workflows by making model predictions transparent and actionable for medical professionals.

Optimizations such as 128D patch embedding dimensions, layer normalization before attention/MLP operations, and a compressed classification head contributed to higher performance and training stability compared to vanilla ViT architectures on small-to-medium-sized datasets.
Random over-sampling was effective in addressing class imbalance by enhancing the minority class (Melanoma) samples to match the majority class (Nevus).

The study highlights that interpretability is as critical as accuracy in the medical domain, as visual explanations help clinicians identify relevant biomarkers and regions of interest in skin lesion analysis.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a binary classification task (Melanoma vs. Melanocytic Nevus) using only the HAM10000 dataset, excluding other lesion types present in the dataset (e.g., Benign Keratosis, BCC, SCC), which limits generalizability to broader skin cancer diagnosis.

The dataset was artificially balanced using random over-sampling of the minority class (Melanoma) to match the majority class (Nevus), which may not reflect real-world clinical prevalence and could lead to over-optimistic performance estimates.

The Vision Transformer model was trained from scratch without transfer learning, which is a significant limitation given the relatively small size of the filtered dataset and the typical data-hungry nature of ViTs.
No external validation was performed; results are reported solely on the HAM10000 dataset, raising concerns about the model's robustness and generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) for the reported metrics (81.3% accuracy, 91.28% AUC), limiting the strength of the evidence for performance claims.
While SHAP was used for XGBoost, specific quantitative SHAP metrics or feature importance values are not fully detailed in the provided text, limiting reproducibility for that component.

The study acknowledges that encoded categorical variables (e.g., dx_type_onehot) pose interpretability challenges for non-experts, indicating a gap between technical XAI outputs and clinical usability.

The paper is a conference publication (INCET 2025) with a relatively small author team from a single institution, which may introduce potential bias or lack of diverse expert review compared to larger multi-center studies.

Data leakage risks are not explicitly addressed; specifically, it is unclear if the random over-sampling was applied before or after train-test split, which could lead to data leakage if samples from the same patient or image source appear in both sets.

The model's performance (81.3% accuracy) is moderate for skin cancer classification tasks, and the paper does not compare these results against state-of-the-art baselines or other recent studies on the same dataset, making it difficult to assess relative superiority.
